images.caption:
Figure 1:Overview of SIS-Bench. Representative evaluation tasks are
illustrated on the left. The right compares human performance with six
proprietary models, where the purple-labeled axes represent spatial
cognition tasks and the orange-labeled axes represent self-awareness
tasks across perception, memory, and reasoning.

Figure 2:Task illustrations of SIS-Bench. The benchmark evaluates 13 tasks under two dimensions (Spatial Cognition and Self-Awareness) and three cognitive levels. Perception is built fromSingle Videoinputs, Memory fromConcatenated Videoinputs, and Reasoning fromLong VideoandShuffled Videoinputs, reflecting progressively higher cognitive demands. Colored boxes are for illustration only and are not part of the model input.

Figure 3:Overview of the SIS-Bench construction pipeline and statistics. The upper panel illustrates the four-stage protocol: Data Processing, Task-specific Annotation, QA Construction, and Dual-expert Verification. The lower panel details dataset statistics, including video-source composition, duration, and task-wise distribution across 13 categories.

header: 4How Well Do MLLMs Handle Self-Awareness and Spatial Cognition in UAV Scenarios?
5A Motion-aware Exploration of Self in Space

images.caption: Figure 4:Overview of the SIS-Motion framework. The architecture (left) comprises parallel encoders for synchronized visual and motion processing. The internal mechanism of the Motion Encoder is further elaborated on the right, showing the structural alignment of motion features into unified tokens compatible with large-scale language models.
Figure 5:Task distribution of SIS-Motion-54K across three formats: MCQ, Open-ended QA, and Description.

Figure 6:Scenarios of the downstream UAV navigation task. Representative samples evaluate the drone’s decision-making and path-planning capabilities by requiring the model to choose the optimal flight sequence to reach a designated target.

Figure 7:Representative source videos used in SIS-Bench construction. Each row shows five temporally sampled frames from one example source video, with frame indices overlaid to indicate their original positions. From top to bottom, AirScape provides short action-centric clips, UrbanVideo-Bench provides long real-world urban flights with substantial viewpoint change, and VisDrone provides low-altitude urban street observations collected from frame sequences.

Figure 8:Representative examples of the four processed video types in SIS-Bench. From top to bottom:Single Videokeeps one original clip and is shown with uniformly sampled frames;Concatenated Videostitches multiple short clips while preserving the order within each clip;Shuffled Videoreorders several segments from a longer trajectory, and the segment labels indicate the presented order;Long Videopreserves the original temporal order of a long flight. Frame indices are overlaid to show the sampling positions in the final processed video.

Figure 9:Statistics of the processed benchmark videos. Left: duration distribution across AirScape, UrbanVideo-Bench, and VisDrone, showing that AirScape mainly contributes short clips while UrbanVideo-Bench and VisDrone provide longer sequences. Center: composition of the processed video pool by construction type, including 689Single Videosamples, 467Concatenated Videosamples, 237Long Videosamples, and 253Shuffled Videosamples. Right: top-20 scene words, illustrating the diversity of benchmark environments.
Figure 10:Unified annotation and verification platform used for expert annotation in Pipeline-C and subsequent dual-expert QA verification.

Figure 11:Annotation template used during metadata construction, illustrated with theRelative Directiontask. We design task-specific templates for both the model-assisted and expert-assisted pipelines so that the collected metadata remains structured and directly usable for later QA construction.

Figure 12:QA-generation template, illustrated with theRelative Directiontask. We design one template for each of the 13 tasks and use multiple LLMs to improve the diversity of question phrasing and distractor construction while preserving answer uniqueness.

Figure 13:Controls for potential confounding factors. (a) Uniform 32-frame sampling. (b) Repeated random shuffling. (c) Chain-of-Thought prompting. (d) Tree-of-Thought prompting. Values report accuracy changes relative to the default evaluation.
Figure 14:Diagnostic error breakdown over 7,987 incorrect model responses. Left: task-wise distributions of dominant error types. Right: overall error distribution.

Figure 15:Representative failures inAction Recognition(top),Action Sequence(middle), andAction Recall(bottom), highlighting weak motion grounding, temporal binding, and rotation–translation discrimination.
